narrative:
Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school and the bar exam ahead, only to hear the model sees a 64% chance of not passing the bar, feels like a gut punch. Picture the admissions team reviewing your file like chapters in your story so far: your first-year grades landed you in the bottom decile of your class, the single strongest signal that whispered early struggles with the rigors of legal study. That pattern held through year three, again placing you in the lowest decile, reinforcing a narrative of consistent challenges in keeping pace with demanding coursework. Your LSAT score of 27 added another layer, suggesting the analytical hurdles of law school might prove tough from the start. Coming from a middle-income family, without the extra safety nets higher brackets sometimes provide, this edged the picture toward steeper odds. Your background as a Black woman, while a testament to your resilience in breaking barriers, statistically aligns with groups the model sees facing disproportionate headwinds on the bar. Even your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.6, your full-time commitment, and other details couldn't fully counterbalance these threads weaving toward difficulty. Together, they painted a cautious portrait—not a judgment on your potential or heart, but a data-driven glimpse of risks that led to this tough call. You've already climbed far with that 3.6 GPA; imagine channeling that grit into targeted prep for grades and tests. This isn't the end of your story—many rewrite predictions through deliberate steps. Keep pushing; the bar waits for those who adapt and rise. With encouragement,  
Your Admissions Guide
